tool.name: recetox-xMSannotator: Compute Peak Correlations
tool.description: compute correlation matrix between peaks based on intensity profiles

configfiles.configfile:
# Load peak table with intensities peak_table <- load_table("$peak_table", "$peak_table.ext")
# Ensure we have a peak identifier column (use 'id' if 'peak' doesn't exist) if (!"peak" %in% names(peak_table) && "id" %in% names(peak_table)) { peak_table$peak <- peak_table$id }
# Extract intensity columns (all columns except peak, mz, rt, id) intensity_cols <- setdiff(names(peak_table), c("peak", "mz", "rt", "id"))
if (length(intensity_cols) == 0) { stop("No intensity columns found in peak table. Expected sample columns.") }
# Create intensity matrix with peak IDs as row names # Note: compute_peak_correlations computes correlations between COLUMNS # So we transpose to get peak-to-peak correlations peak_intensity_matrix <- as.matrix(peak_table[, intensity_cols]) rownames(peak_intensity_matrix) <- as.character(peak_table$peak)
# Compute correlation matrix (transposed so peaks are columns) peak_correlation_matrix <- recetox.xmsannotator::compute_peak_correlations( t(peak_intensity_matrix), correlation_method = "$correlation_method" )
# Convert to data frame and preserve row names as a column cor_df <- as.data.frame(peak_correlation_matrix) cor_df$peak <- rownames(cor_df) # Reorder to put peak column first cor_df <- cor_df[, c("peak", setdiff(names(cor_df), "peak"))]
# Save output save_table(cor_df, "$output_file", "$output_file.ext")

param.name: peak_table
param.label: Peak table with intensities
param.help: Table containing peak information and intensity values across samples. Required columns: peak (or id), plus one or more sample intensity columns. Typically output from recetox-aplcms or similar tool.

param.name: correlation_method
param.label: Correlation method
param.help: Method for computing correlations between peak intensity profiles.

tool.help:
**Compute Peak Correlations**
Calculate pairwise correlation coefficients between peaks based on their intensity profiles across samples.
Description ===========
This tool computes a correlation matrix where each entry represents the correlation coefficient between two peaks' intensity profiles across all samples. Peaks derived from the same parent compound (e.g., different adducts or isotopes) typically show high correlation because they co-vary across samples.
Correlation Methods -------------------
**Pearson correlation (default)** Measures linear correlation between intensity profiles. Best when relationships are approximately linear. Recommended for most use cases.
**Spearman rank correlation** Measures monotonic (not necessarily linear) relationships. More robust to outliers. Use when intensity distributions are non-normal.
**Kendall rank correlation** Another rank-based method, more computationally intensive but statistically efficient for smaller datasets.
Inputs ------
**Peak Table with Intensities** A table containing peak identifiers and intensity values across multiple samples. Sample columns should be named consistently (e.g., sample names from your experiment).
**Correlation Method** Choose the statistical method for computing correlations. See descriptions above.
Outputs -------
**Correlation Matrix** A square matrix where entry [i,j] is the correlation coefficient between peak i and peak j. Values range from -1 (perfect negative correlation) to +1 (perfect positive correlation). Diagonal entries are always 1.0.
Usage Notes -----------
This tool is typically followed by: - **Compute Peak Modules**: Groups highly correlated peaks into modules - The correlation threshold parameter in Compute Peak Modules determines which correlations are considered significant
Example Workflow ----------------
1. Simple Annotation → Initial compound matching 2. Compute Peak Correlations → Calculate peak relationships 3. Compute Peak Modules → Group correlated peaks 4. Continue with RT modules, isotopes, etc.
Performance Considerations --------------------------
For large datasets (>10,000 peaks), correlation computation can be memory-intensive. Consider: - Filtering low-intensity peaks before running - Using Galaxy's job resource parameters to allocate sufficient memory - Spearman/Kendall methods are slower than Pearson for large datasets
References ----------
Zhang, B., & Horvath, S. (2005). A General Framework for Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis. Statistical Applications in Genetics and Molecular Biology, 4(1). DOI: 10.2202/1544-6115.1128
